This figure displays the movement module 924. The process begins with the movement module 924 being initiated, at step 1200, by the base module 920. The movement module 924 receives, at step 1202, the extracted data entry from the base module 920. For example, the movement module 924 receives the data entry from the base module 920, which contains the data for the patient ID, the type of surgery, the 3D image of the patient's medical images, the starting position of the surgical robot 902, the ending location of the surgical robot 902, the movement data file which contains the movements required by the surgical robot 902 to move from the starting position to the ending position, the process required for the surgery, the techniques required for the surgery, the tools required for the surgery, the data file for the procedure or technique including the data files for replays of the step as inputted into the CAD GUI 942 and the x, y, z coordinates of the patient's body and the tools used, the techniques used, such as the threading technique used in the surgery, the data files for the robotic movements of the process or techniques, the calculations, such as the forces required in certain steps or techniques, the materials required for certain steps or techniques, and the specialists required for the specific steps or techniques. The movement module 924 determines, at step 1204, the current location of the surgical robot 902. For example, the movement module 924 may determine the current location of the surgical robot 902 by using sensors of mobility system 918, such as image recognition devices, ultrasonic sensors, infrared sensors, etc. Mobility system 918 is sometimes referred to as a “mobile unit.” An example mobile unit 446 is illustrated with reference to FIG. 4A. The mobile unit 446 can include actuators, wheels 444, and a motor 442. Example wheels 444 and an example motor 442 are illustrated with reference to FIG. 4A. Movement module 924 may utilize markers on the operating room floor to determine the current location of the surgical robot 902, such as at docking station, approaching top of the operating table, approaching the bottom of the operating table, approaching left side of the operating table, approaching right side of the operating table, etc. while continuously scanning using the sensors of the mobility system 918 to ensure that the surgical robot 902 will not bump or run into anything in the operating room. For example, the surgical robot may receive user inputs for the position required for the surgical tool or end effectors and not receive user inputs to position itself throughout the operating room by using the sensors 916 and the mobility system 918. In embodiments, navigating mobility system 918 includes causing mobility system 918 to determine a path to at least one of a portion of an anatomy of a patient or an instrument using at least one of quick response (QR) codes, labels, or arrows posted on a surface of an operating room. For example, there may be markers on the ground, such as QR codes, labels, arrows, etc. to identify a path to certain areas of the patient in which the surgical robot 902 reads the markers and can navigate the operating room to a final position either near or away from the patient depending on the need of the surgical robot 902. For example, if the surgical robot is needed to be on the left side of the patient near the patient's leg there may be markers leading to the left side of the patient and once on the left side the markers may signal to the surgical robot the position relative to the patient, such as head level, upper chest level, lower chest level, upper abdomen level, lower abdomen level, hip level, femur level, knee level, chin or calf level, ankle level, foot level, etc. In some embodiments, surgical robot 902 does not need user inputted navigation to locate and relocate around the patient during a surgical procedure in which the surgical robot 902 is needed. For example, a pre-operative surgical plan can indicate positions and orientations for surgical robot 902, a surgical room layout, and changes to positions of a bed or equipment. An example operating room 102 including an surgical bed and example medical equipment is illustrated and described in more detail with reference to FIG. 1. The machine learning module 200 (illustrated and described in more detail with reference to FIG. 2 ) can be used to generate repositioning instructions for surgical robot 902 to optimize outcomes (determined based on simulations) for surgical steps. A surgeon can indicate that a surgical tool such as an incision knife is required to be used at the left ankle of the patient and the surgical robot 902 would navigate the operating room using the markers to locate the left side of the patient and the relocate to the ankle level of the patient using the markers next to the operating table. In some embodiments, if the surgical robot 902 is in the wrong location the surgical robot 902 may use the markers to reposition itself to the correct position using the same method. In some embodiments, the user may input the [data?] if the surgical robot 902 is in the correct starting position, such as at a docking station located in the operating room, or the position in relation to the patient to confirm the surgical robot 902 is in the correct location. Then movement module 924 may determine if the current location of the surgical robot 902 is the correct location. For example, the movement module 924 may determine the current location of the surgical robot 902 by using sensors from the mobility system 918 and compare the current location to the starting location stored in the received data entry from the base module 920 stored in the surgery database 928. In some embodiments, the user may input the [data?] if the surgical robot 902 is in the correct starting position, such as at a docking station located in the operating room, or the position in relation to the patient to confirm the surgical robot 902 is in the correct location. If it is determined that the current location of the surgical robot 902 is not correct the movement module 924 may alert the user. For example, if the surgical robot 902 is not in the correct location the movement module 924 may alert a user through a notification, such as a flashing light, a noise, or a message on the CAD GUI 942, etc. Then the user may input the current location of the surgical robot 902 and the movement actions for the surgical robot 902. For example, the user may input the current location of the surgical robot 902 in the CAD GUI 942 and the movements required from the mobility system 918 to have the surgical robot 902 relocate to the desired position in the operating room. The movement module 924 executes, at step 1206, the movement action. For example, the movement module 924 executes the movement data file stored in the surgery database 928 which contains the required movement of the surgical robot 902 through the use of the mobility system 918 to relocate the surgical robot 902 to the required position. In some embodiments, the movement of the surgical robot 902 may be from a data file containing the movement instructions of the surgical robot 902, such as 3 feet straight ahead, rotate 90 degrees, 2 feet straight ahead, etc. In some embodiments, the surgical robot 902 may use markers on the ground, such as QR codes, labels, arrows, etc. to identify a path to certain areas of the patient, such as left side head level, left side torso level, left side leg level, left side feet level, etc. Then the movement module 924 is continuously polling, at step 1208, for obstructions. For example, the movement module 924 is continuously receiving sensor data from the mobility system 918 in order to determine if there are any obstructions in the path of the surgical robot 902. For example, the mobility system 918 may be equipped with a plurality of sensors, such as image recognition devices, ultrasonic sensors, infrared sensors, etc. to detect objects that may be obstructing the surgical robots 902 path. In some embodiments, the mobility system 918 may be able to communicate with other surgical robots 902 or the medical staff to notify if the surgical robots 902 or medical staff are deemed to be an obstruction or if the surgical robot 902 itself is an obstruction. The movement module 924 determines, at step 1210, if an obstruction was detected. For example, the movement module 924 is continuously receiving sensor data from the mobility system 918 in order to determine if there are any obstructions in the path of the surgical robot 902. For example, the mobility system 918 may be equipped with a plurality of sensors, such as image recognition devices, ultrasonic sensors, infrared sensors, etc. to detect objects that may be obstructing the surgical robots 902 path. In some embodiments, the mobility system 918 may be able to communicate with other surgical robots 902 or the medical staff to notify if the surgical robots 902 or medical staff are deemed to be an obstruction or if the surgical robot 902 itself is an obstruction. If it is determined that an obstruction was detected then the movement module 924 alerts, at step 1212, the user. For example, if an obstruction is detected the movement module 924 may alert a user through a notification, such as a flashing light, a noise, a message on the CAD GUI 942, etc. to either alert the user of being an obstruction to the surgical robot 902 or to notify the user that the surgical robot is adjusting its course based on another type of obstruction, such as a medical light, table, chair, etc. In embodiments, surgical robot 902 detects an obstruction to robotic arm 912 while surgical robot 902 is performing a surgical step. Surgical robot 902 indicates presence of the obstruction. Surgical robot 902 later determines absence of the obstruction. S.R.N 934 causes surgical robot 902 to continue performing the surgical step. If it is determined that an obstruction was not detected or after the user has been alerted and removed the obstruction the movement module 924 continues, at step 1214, executing the movement action. For example, the movement module 924 executes the movement data file stored in the surgery database 928 which contains the required movement of the surgical robot 902 through the use of the mobility system 918 to relocate the surgical robot 902 to the required position. If it is determined that the movement action is not complete, then the movement module 924 continues to poll for obstructions. If it is determined that the movement action is complete the movement module 924 sends, at step 1218, the completion status to the procedure module 926. For example, once the movement is complete the movement module 924 sends a completion status to the procedure module 926 in order to allow the procedure module 926 to extract the procedure data file and perform the necessary actions of the surgery. In some embodiments, the completion status may be stored in the surgery database 928. The movement module 924 returns, at step 9220, to the base module 920. The procedure module 926 determines, at step 1304, if the completion status was received from the movement module 924. In some embodiments, the completion status may be stored in the surgery database 928. If the procedure module 926 determines that the completion status was not received from the movement module 924 then the procedure module 926 is continuously polling, at step 1306, to receive the completion status from the movement module 924 and returns to receiving the data entry from the base module 920. If the completion status was received then the procedure module 926 receives, at step 1308, the completion status from the movement module 924. For example, the procedure module 926 receives the completion status from the movement module 924. In some embodiments, the completion status may be stored in the surgery database 928. The procedure module 926 extracts, at step 1310, the procedure data file from the data entry. For example, the procedure module 926 extracts the process or technique stored in the surgery database 928, such as mark spots on skin for anchors and suture locations on extensor retinaculum, make incision at distal point of fibula, use forceps to open incision, separate extensor retinaculum from fibula, etc. In some embodiments, the process or techniques are stored in order so that the extracted process or technique is the next step in an operation. The procedure module 926 sends, at step 1312, the procedure data file to the end effector 914. For example, the procedure module 926 sends the data file that contains the necessary movements of the end effector 914 to perform the necessary action of the surgery. The procedure module 926 executes, at step 1314, the procedure data file. For example, for a procedure to stabilize an ankle the process stored in the surgery database 928 may be that the patient is prepped and draped. Arthroscopic debridement takes place of the ankle joint. An incision is made from the tip of the fibula to the extensor retinaculum. A drill hole is made into the calcaneus and an anchor is placed. Suture is then sewn into the extensor retinaculum. A drill hole is made into the fibula and an anchor is placed. The suture limbs from the anchors are tied to bring the ankle mortise into neutral position to stabilize the ankle joint. For example, for the step of marking the location of the surgery the user may use a surgical skin marker tool in order to identify the specific locations on the patient for the incisions and other procedures such as the location of sutures, for example for a Brostrom-Gould repair surgery. For example, the robotic arm 912 with an end effector 914 equipped with an incision knife may make an incision at the distal point of the fibula using the data file that contains the x, y, z coordinates of the patient's ankle as well as the necessary movements of the robotic arm 912. For example, the user may physically position a drill guide to determine the location of the anchors, and the end effector 914 with the drill may use the drill guide to insert the drill into the fibula and the end effector 914 with the drill is removed from the ankle leaving a location in the fibula for the anchor to be placed. Another end effector 914 with the anchor may use the drill guide to place an anchor in the fibula and when exiting the drill guide the end effector may hold the end of the suture, and the process may be repeated to place an anchor in the calcaneus bone. Then another end effector with a suture lasso may weave in and out of the extensor retinaculum ligament to create the suture holes for the suture from the anchor and a separate end effector 914 may loop the suture from the anchor into the suture lasso, which allows the end effector 914 with the suture lasso to pull leaving the suture from the anchor to be woven into the extensor retinaculum ligament, and this process may be repeated to reattach the ligament back to the bone. In some embodiments, the robotic arm 912 with end effectors 914 may perform the majority of the surgery. In some embodiments, the robotic arm 912 may be there to assist the surgeon or physician, such as drilling the holes for the anchors, placing the anchors, inserting the sutures into the ligament, etc. The procedure module 926 returns, at step 1316, to the base module 920. In some embodiments, the database may contain staging areas for surgical robots 902 and active working areas and their corresponding range of motion. In some embodiments, the base and robotic arm 912 may be capable of moving independently such that when the base moves, the robotic arm can maintain its position and orientation, allowing the surgical robot 902 to make room for additional equipment or personnel or to increase the reach of the robot while not disrupting the procedure. In some embodiments, the surgical robots 902 may coordinate with multiple routines which allow the surgical robots 902 to stage out of the way and approach the operating table only when required. Routines may be preprogramed and triggered by a surgeon and the surgical robots 902 may navigate around the surgical staff and other equipment and may request other movable surgical robots 902 and surgical staff to move if necessary. In some embodiments, if the user, such as the surgeon or physician, adjusts or updates the process or technique then the updated process or technique will be stored in the surgery database 928. Suture is then sewn into the extensor retinaculum. A drill hole is made into the fibula and an anchor is placed. The suture limbs from the anchors are tied to bring the ankle mortise into neutral position to stabilize the ankle joint. Example cameras, sensors, and an operating room 102 are illustrated and described in more detail with reference to FIG. 1. The user may input the ending location of the surgical robot 902 in relation to the patient, such as left side head level, left side torso level, left side leg level, left side foot level, etc. The user inputs may be stored as a data file for the instructions or program for mobility system 918 to position the surgical robot 902 in the correct position to perform the functions or action of the procedure required by the surgical robot 902. The mobility system 918 may comprises the elements required for movement, such as wheels 444 and at least one motor 442. The wheels 444 may be omnidirectional, tracks, etc. It may further comprise dedicated sensors, processing abilities, etc. and communicate with the rest of the surgical robot 902 systems to enable coordination with the at least one robotic arm. invention_title.text: COMPUTERIZED CONTROL AND NAVIGATION OF A ROBOTIC SURGICAL APPARATUS

calculation_log: Earliest Filing Date: 2023-05-22 priority to US202318321084A

abstract.text: The present disclosure provides a method for a surgical robot with extended operational range in which a user inputs the surgical robot's movements and locations in a CAD environment through a surgical robot network and the surgical robot movements and locations are stored as a data file. The surgical robot network sends the surgical robot movements and locations data files to the surgical robot which extracts the data file containing the surgical robot movements and locations. The surgical robot then executes the data files containing the surgical robot movements and locations and sends a completion status of the movements and locations and the surgical robot executes a data file containing the robotic movements to perform aspects of the surgical procedure.

claim_text: 1. A computer-implemented method for control of a robotic surgical apparatus, the computer-implemented method comprising: determining one or more instruments are available for performing at least a portion of a surgical step to be performed on a portion of an anatomy of a patient by the robotic surgical apparatus; determining at least one outcome of a simulation with the one or more instruments and the robotic surgical apparatus when the robotic surgical apparatus is positioned at a first location in an operating room, wherein the simulation indicates that an end effector of the robotic surgical apparatus is unable to use the one or more instruments to perform the portion of the surgical step; determining a second location in the operating room for the robotic surgical apparatus based on the surgical step; and after the robotic surgical apparatus is moved from the first location to the second location, causing the robotic surgical apparatus to perform the surgical step at the second location using the end effector and the one or more instruments.
2. The computer-implemented method of claim 1, further comprising moving at least a portion of the robotic surgical apparatus to the second location using a motorized mobile unit.

3. The computer-implemented method of claim 2, wherein moving the at least the portion of the robotic surgical apparatus comprises: causing a robotic arm of the robotic surgical apparatus and the end effector to remain stationary while another portion of the robotic surgical apparatus moves from the first location to the second location, and/or moving a table supporting the patient relative to the robotic arm.
4. The computer-implemented method of claim 2, wherein the motorized mobile unit comprises at least one of: an actuator, a wheel, a motor, or a sensor.

5. The computer-implemented method of claim 2, further comprising: causing the motorized mobile unit to communicate with a robotic arm of the robotic surgical apparatus to coordinate movement of the motorized mobile unit with positioning of the robotic arm.

6. The computer-implemented method of claim 2, further comprising: causing the motorized mobile unit to move the at least the portion of the robotic surgical apparatus or the one or more instruments using at least one of quick response (QR) codes, labels, or arrows posted on a surface of the operating room.
7. The computer-implemented method of claim 1, further comprising generating, using a machine learning module, instructions to move the robotic surgical apparatus based on the surgical step.

8. A system comprising: one or more processors; and one or more memories storing instructions that, when executed by the one or more processors, cause the system to perform a process for control and navigation of a robotic surgical apparatus, the process comprising: determining one or more instruments are available for performing at least a portion of a surgical step to be performed on a portion of an anatomy of a patient by the robotic surgical apparatus; determining at least one outcome of a simulation with the one or more instruments and the robotic surgical apparatus when the robotic surgical apparatus is positioned at a first location in an operating room, wherein the simulation indicates that an end effector of the robotic surgical apparatus is unable to use the one or more instruments to perform the portion of the surgical step; determining a second location in the operating room for the robotic surgical apparatus based on the surgical step; and after the robotic surgical apparatus is moved from the first location to the second location, causing the robotic surgical apparatus to perform the surgical step at the second location using the end effector and the one or more instruments.
9. The system of claim 8, wherein the process further comprises: moving at least a portion of the robotic surgical apparatus to the second location using a motorized mobile unit.

10. The system of claim 9, wherein moving the at least the portion of the robotic surgical apparatus comprises: causing a robotic arm of the robotic surgical apparatus and the end effector to remain stationary while another portion of the robotic surgical apparatus moves from the first location to the second location, and/or moving a table supporting the patient relative to the robotic arm.
11. The system of claim 9, wherein the motorized mobile unit comprises at least one of: an actuator, a wheel, a motor, or a sensor.

12. The system of claim 9, wherein the process further comprises: causing the motorized mobile unit to communicate with a robotic arm of the robotic surgical apparatus to coordinate movement of the motorized mobile unit with positioning of the robotic arm.

13. The system of claim 9, wherein the process further comprises: causing the motorized mobile unit to move at least a portion of the robotic surgical apparatus or the one or more instruments using at least one of quick response (QR) codes, labels, or arrows posted on a surface of the operating room.
14. The system of claim 8, wherein the process further comprises: generating, using a machine learning module, instructions to move the robotic surgical apparatus based on the surgical step.

15. A non-transitory computer-readable medium storing instructions that, when executed by a computing system, cause the computing system to perform operations comprising: determining one or more instruments are available for performing at least a portion of a surgical step to be performed on a portion of an anatomy of a patient by a robotic surgical apparatus; determining at least one outcome of a simulation with the one or more instruments and the robotic surgical apparatus when the robotic surgical apparatus is positioned at a first location in an operating room, wherein the simulation indicates that an end effector of the robotic surgical apparatus is unable to use the one or more instruments to perform the portion of the surgical step; determining a second location in the operating room for the robotic surgical apparatus based on the surgical step; and after the robotic surgical apparatus is moved from the first location to the second location, causing the robotic surgical apparatus to perform the surgical step at the second location using the end effector and the one or more instruments.
